serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw watercress is approximately 34 g (1 cup chopped), providing about 4 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Watercress, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11591)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw watercress used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA data and are retained from the input record. Saturated fat and additional micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 11591.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — Analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate leafy greens category; reference: Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002. Am J Clin Nutr. 2002;76(1):5-56.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw watercress exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the established GI range for non-starchy leafy green vegetables (GI 10–20) as documented in Foster-Powell et al. (2002) and consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate leafy greens. Glycemic load per 34 g serving = (15 × 1.29 × 34/100) / 100 ≈ 0.07, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (41 mg/100 g) and high potassium (330 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Watercress is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium content and high potassium level (330 mg/100 g) support the DASH dietary pattern and contribute to blood pressure reduction through improved sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.29 g/100 g) and negligible glycaemic impact (estimated GI ~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Watercress is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its extremely low carbohydrate content (1.29 g/100 g), low estimated glycaemic index (~15), and negligible glycaemic load per serving make it a safe, nutrient-dense leafy green that does not meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant phytochemicals including glucosinolates and vitamin C
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Watercress is cardioprotective; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium (330 mg/100 g), and rich antioxidant content (vitamin C 43 mg/100 g, vitamin E 1 mg/100 g, glucosinolates) collectively support vascular health and are consistent with cardiovascular disease prevention dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory glucosinolates, isothiocyanates, and vitamin C
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Watercress contains sulforaphane precursors and isothiocyanates with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and its high vitamin C content (43 mg/100 g) supports collagen synthesis and immune modulation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Watercress (Nasturtium officinale) is a semi-aquatic, fast-growing leafy green vegetable belonging to the Brassicaceae (cruciferous) family. In its raw form, it consists of small, dark-green rounded leaves on thin, tender stems with a distinctly peppery, slightly bitter, and mildly spicy flavour profile reminiscent of mustard greens. It is consumed raw in salads, sandwiches, and garnishes, or briefly cooked in soups and stir-fries. Nutritionally, raw watercress is exceptionally low in calories (11 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (1.29 g/100 g), making it one of the most nutrient-dense foods by calorie. It provides meaningful protein (2.3 g/100 g) for a leafy green, negligible fat (0.1 g/100 g), and is rich in vitamin C (43 mg/100 g), vitamin A (160 mcg RAE/100 g), potassium (330 mg/100 g), and calcium (120 mg/100 g). Its glucosinolate content — particularly gluconasturtiin, which converts to phenethyl isothiocyanate — confers anti-inflammatory and potential chemoprotective properties. Clinically, watercress is suitable for individuals managing hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (negligible glycaemic impact, estimated GI ~15), and cardiovascular disease (zero cholesterol, minimal saturated fat, antioxidant-rich). It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Watercress is widely cultivated across Europe, Asia, and the Americas and is used in British, French, Chinese, and Southeast Asian cuisines.
common_user_queries: Is watercress good for high blood pressure?
How many calories in raw watercress?
What are the health benefits of watercress?
How much protein does watercress have?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95 per input record) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 11591 for raw watercress. Both sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Additional micronutrient values drawn directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source (Foster-Powell et al. 2002) as no direct GI measurement exists for watercress.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g; this is confirmed zero per both Singapore HPB and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — watercress is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 2. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero as watercress is a plant food with no animal-derived components. 3. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for raw watercress. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero for an unprocessed raw leafy vegetable. 5. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for raw watercress is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. The estimate is based on the established GI range for non-starchy leafy green vegetables per Foster-Powell et al. (2002). 6. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are cited as defensible matches; reviewers should verify FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301502 and SNOMED CT 227316009 against current ontology releases. FoodEx2 code A00LC is cited as the watercress entry in the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system. 7. Watercress is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Local names fields are set to null accordingly. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy and are populated with numeric values; no -1 sentinel values are used for these fields.
